1.6.a.info:
An IPv4 address is a 32-bit logical identifier assigned to every device (host, router interface, server NIC) that participates in an IP network. It operates at Layer 3 of the OSI model and is used by routers to make forwarding decisions about which network a packet should travel to next.

Humans rarely see the raw 32 bits. Instead, IPv4 addresses are written in dotted-decimal notation: four 8-bit octets separated by periods, each octet in the range 0–255. The canonical example 192.168.1.10 in binary is 11000000.10101000.00000001.00001010.

Every IPv4 address carries two embedded concepts: a network portion (which subnet the host belongs to) and a host portion (which specific device inside that subnet). The dividing line is defined by the subnet mask, not by the address itself. Two hosts with the same network portion and different host portions can communicate directly; otherwise, they must go through a router.

  • Format: 4 octets × 8 bits = 32 total bits
  • Range per octet: 0–255 (2^8 values)
  • Layer: OSI Layer 3 / TCP-IP Internet Layer
  • Uniqueness: must be unique on its routing scope (public Internet-wide for public IPs, internal for private IPs + NAT)

hack.memory: IPv4 = 4 numbers, each 0–255, totaling 32 bits. Think of it as a street address Layer 3 routers use to find your house. Not a phone number (that's the port), not a fingerprint (that's the MAC) — an address that can change when you move networks.
hack.practice: Write 5 random IPs (e.g., 172.20.5.87) on paper and convert each to 32-bit binary. Open Packet Tracer, put an IP on a PC, then run ipconfig /all and confirm the mask and gateway. Repeat daily until conversion is automatic.
hack.meta: Foundational. Every single subnetting question assumes you can read and mentally decompose a dotted-decimal IP. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 12 opens with this exact concept.

1.6.a.info:
An octet is a group of 8 bits — the building block of an IPv4 address. An IPv4 address has exactly four octets separated by dots, so the address is always 4 × 8 = 32 bits long.

Because each octet is 8 bits, it has 2^8 = 256 possible values, ranging from 0 to 255. Anything outside this range (e.g., 192.168.1.256) is an invalid IPv4 address. The bit positional values inside one octet are the classic 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 (always sum to 255 when every bit is 1).

Some texts call octets 'bytes.' In networking the term octet is preferred because 'byte' historically was not always 8 bits on every platform — RFCs standardize on 'octet' for precision.

  • 00000000 = 0 (minimum)
  • 11111111 = 255 (maximum)
  • 10000000 = 128 (only the 128-bit set)

hack.memory: Octet = 8 (octagon, octopus, octave). 8 bits, 4 of them per IPv4 address = 32 total. Max value 255 because 128+64+32+16+8+4+2+1 = 255. If you ever see an IP with an octet > 255, it's fake / a typo / a trick question.
hack.practice: Drill 10 random decimals (0–255) → 8-bit binary and 10 random 8-bit binaries → decimal. Target under 10 seconds each. Use subnettingpractice.com's octet drill.
hack.meta: Knowing the octet structure cold lets you instantly spot invalid addresses on the exam (common distractor).

1.6.a.info:
Every IPv4 address is logically split into a network portion and a host portion. The subnet mask (or CIDR prefix length) defines where the split happens — the left-most bits marked as 1 in the mask are the network portion; the right-most bits marked as 0 are the host portion.

With a /24 mask (255.255.255.0): the first 24 bits identify the network, and the last 8 bits identify the host. For 192.168.1.10/24, the network is 192.168.1.0 and the host is .10.

Devices on the same network portion can talk directly (same Layer 2 broadcast domain). Devices with a different network portion must send their packets to a default gateway (router) to be forwarded. This is the whole reason routers exist.

  • Routers compare the destination IP's network portion against their routing table
  • The subnet mask is the contract that tells every device where the split is
  • Changing the mask changes where the network/host boundary falls

hack.memory: Subnet mask = the fence. Everything left of the fence is the neighborhood (network). Everything right of the fence is the specific house (host). Move the fence → different size neighborhoods.
hack.practice: Take 10.20.30.40 with masks /8, /16, /24, /30 and write out the network and host portions for each. Watch how the boundary slides.
hack.meta: This is the mental model every subnetting question requires. Miss this and every /26, /28, /30 problem becomes guesswork.

1.6.a.info:
Inside a single octet, each bit has a fixed positional value based on powers of 2. Reading left to right, the 8 values are: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. These are 2^7 down to 2^0 and they always sum to 255.

To convert a binary octet to decimal, add the positional values of every 1-bit. For 11001010: 128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 202. To convert decimal to binary, subtract the largest fitting positional value, mark a 1, repeat.

These same positional values are the only numbers that can ever appear in a valid subnet mask octet: 0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255. Each is the running sum of 1-bits from the left: 128; 128+64=192; 128+64+32=224; and so on.

  • Bit 7 (leftmost) = 128
  • Bit 0 (rightmost) = 1
  • All 8 bits set = 128+64+32+16+8+4+2+1 = 255

hack.memory: Magic 8: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Each halves the previous. Sum = 255. Write these across your exam whiteboard the moment you sit down — they answer every binary question.
hack.practice: On paper: convert the decimals 200, 172, 240, 19, 89 to 8-bit binary using only the positional values. Time each conversion — aim for under 10 seconds.
hack.meta: Every binary conversion on the CCNA starts here. You cannot subnet without these numbers memorized cold.

1.6.a.info:
The network address is the address in which all host bits are set to 0. It identifies the subnet itself, not a specific device. Network addresses are routable (they appear in routing tables) but are not assignable to any host interface.

Example: In 192.168.1.0/24, the host portion is the last 8 bits. If you set them all to 0, the resulting address 192.168.1.0 is the network address. Any packet addressed to 192.168.1.0 is interpreted as 'the network' and is never delivered to a host.

Finding the network address from an IP is the single most common subnetting task: apply a bitwise AND of the IP with the mask, or round the interesting octet down to the nearest multiple of the block size.

  • Bit pattern: network bits unchanged, host bits all 0
  • Not usable by hosts — it is the subnet's 'name tag'
  • Appears in show ip route as a connected route's network

hack.memory: Network address = the street name sign. You don't mail a package to 'Maple Street' — you mail it to a specific house on Maple Street. All host bits = 0 → unassignable.
hack.practice: Take 10.1.77.55/20 and find the network address using block size: 256−240 = 16, 77 falls in the 64 block, so network = 10.1.64.0. Repeat for 10 different IP/mask pairs.
hack.meta: Every subnetting question starts with 'what is the network address?' Get fast at the block-size trick.

1.6.a.info:
The broadcast address is the address in which all host bits are set to 1. A packet sent to the broadcast address is delivered to every host within that subnet. Like the network address, it is routable but not assignable to an individual host.

For 192.168.1.0/24, the host bits are the last 8 bits. Setting them all to 1 gives 192.168.1.255 — the directed broadcast. Hosts answer ARP, DHCP Discover, and many legacy protocols based on broadcast.

Do not confuse the directed broadcast (192.168.1.255) with the limited broadcast (255.255.255.255). Limited broadcast never leaves the local link; directed broadcast historically could be routed, though this is disabled by default on modern routers (no ip directed-broadcast).

  • Bit pattern: network bits unchanged, host bits all 1
  • Reaches every host on the subnet simultaneously
  • Reduces usable host count by 1 (that's the −2 in the formula)

hack.memory: Broadcast = megaphone to the whole block. All host bits = 1 → every device in the subnet hears it. Can't be assigned to any single device because it belongs to everyone.
hack.practice: For 172.16.32.0/20: block size 16 in the third octet, network 172.16.32.0, broadcast = next network − 1 = 172.16.47.255. Drill 10 of these.
hack.meta: Together with network address, this is half of every subnetting question.

1.6.a.info:
The bitwise AND operation is how a router extracts the network address from any IP + mask pair. Rule: 1 AND 1 = 1, everything else = 0. Applying AND between each bit of the IP and the corresponding bit of the mask produces the network address.

Example: 192.168.1.130 AND 255.255.255.192:

IP   : 11000000.10101000.00000001.10000010
Mask : 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000
AND  : 11000000.10101000.00000001.10000000  → 192.168.1.128

Because AND with 1 keeps the bit and AND with 0 zeroes it out, the mask effectively 'passes through' the network bits and 'zeroes' the host bits — which is exactly the definition of a network address. Routers perform this operation billions of times per second in hardware (TCAM) on every forwarded packet.

  • IP bit AND mask bit 1 → keeps the bit
  • IP bit AND mask bit 0 → zeroes the bit (host portion)
  • Result is always the network address

hack.memory: AND = keep the 1s from both sides. The mask's 1s are a cookie cutter — whatever falls under a 1 survives, whatever falls under a 0 dies. What survives = network address.
hack.meta: Understanding AND demystifies every subnetting shortcut. The block-size method is just a fast substitute for AND.

1.6.a.info:
The total IPv4 address space is 2^32 = 4,294,967,296 addresses — roughly 4.3 billion. When IPv4 was designed in the 1970s, 4 billion addresses felt infinite; today it is nowhere near enough.

IANA exhausted its free pool of /8 blocks in February 2011. Regional Internet Registries (ARIN, RIPE, APNIC, LACNIC, AFRINIC) ran out over the next several years. Today, new IPv4 allocations come only from reclaimed space or an expensive secondary market (~$30–50 per address in 2026).

Two responses to exhaustion:

  • NAT (Network Address Translation) — lets millions of private hosts share a few public IPs. Extended IPv4's life by decades.
  • IPv6 — 128-bit addresses, 2^128 ≈ 3.4 × 10^38 total, fundamentally solves exhaustion.

Not all 4.3B are usable: Class D (multicast), Class E (experimental), 0.0.0.0/8 and 127.0.0.0/8 reservations, and RFC 1918 private ranges all carve out chunks, leaving roughly 3.7B usable public addresses.

hack.memory: 4.3 billion sounds huge, but the Internet has more than 4.3 billion phones. NAT was the duct tape; IPv6 is the real fix.
hack.practice: Look up your current ISP's IPv4 block on whois and compute how many addresses it has (/24 = 256, /16 = 65K, /8 = 16M).
hack.meta: Exam tests 'why IPv6?' — the answer is always IPv4 exhaustion plus NAT complexity.

1.6.b.info:
Class A covers IPv4 addresses with a first octet from 1 to 126. Its defining binary signature is a leading 0 in the most significant bit (0xxxxxxx). The default classful mask is /8 (255.0.0.0), meaning the first octet alone is the network portion and the remaining 24 bits are for hosts.

Each Class A network supports 2^24 − 2 = 16,777,214 hosts. There are only 126 possible Class A networks globally (plus 127 which is reserved for loopback). These were assigned mostly in the 1980s to huge institutions (MIT, Ford, IBM, the US military, Apple), many of which still hold them.

Examples: 10.0.0.0/8 (RFC 1918 private), 8.8.8.8 (Google DNS), 1.1.1.1 (Cloudflare DNS). The private block 10.0.0.0/8 is the single most-used internal network range — CoreWeave, AWS, and Azure all default to it.

  • Range: 1.0.0.0 – 126.255.255.255
  • Leading bit: 0
  • Default mask: /8
  • Hosts/network: 16,777,214

params.label: Class A 10.0.0.0/8 — leading bit 0, first octet 1–126

hack.memory: Class A = Absurdly huge. 1–126, /8, leading bit 0. 126 networks, 16.7M hosts each. 'A big nets, A lot of hosts.'
hack.practice: Is 8.8.8.8 Class A? Yes (8 is 1–126). Is 127.0.0.1 Class A? No — it's loopback. Is 126.200.1.1 Class A? Yes.
hack.meta: Memorize the 1–126 cutoff. The 127 gap is a classic exam trap.

1.6.b.info:
Class B covers first octets 128–191 with a leading binary pattern of 10 (10xxxxxx). The default classful mask is /16 (255.255.0.0), so the first two octets form the network and the last two carry the hosts.

Each Class B network provides 2^16 − 2 = 65,534 hosts and there are 16,384 possible Class B networks globally. These were sized for medium-to-large organizations — universities, regional ISPs, medium enterprises.

Examples: 172.16.0.0/16 (within the RFC 1918 172.16.0.0/12 private block), 169.254.0.0/16 (APIPA link-local), 128.32.0.0/16 (UC Berkeley, historical allocation).

  • Range: 128.0.0.0 – 191.255.255.255
  • Leading bits: 10
  • Default mask: /16
  • Hosts/network: 65,534

params.label: Class B 172.16.0.0/16 — leading bits 10, first octet 128–191

hack.memory: Class B = Big (but not huge). 128–191, /16, leading bits 10. 65K hosts. 'B for Boundary — 128 is the binary midpoint (10000000).'
hack.practice: Is 172.20.5.1 Class B? Yes (172 is 128–191). Is 191.255.255.255 Class B? Yes (edge of range). Is 192.0.0.1 Class B? No — it's Class C.
hack.meta: 128 is the Class B start because it's the binary boundary 10000000.

1.6.b.info:
Class C covers first octets 192–223 with a leading binary pattern of 110 (110xxxxx). The default classful mask is /24 (255.255.255.0), so the first three octets define the network and only the last octet is left for hosts.

Each Class C network supports 2^8 − 2 = 254 hosts, and there are 2,097,152 possible Class C networks — a huge number of small networks, perfectly sized for home and SOHO deployments.

Examples: 192.168.1.0/24 (within RFC 1918 192.168.0.0/16 private), 203.0.113.0/24 (TEST-NET-3 documentation range), any public /24 assigned to a small business.

  • Range: 192.0.0.0 – 223.255.255.255
  • Leading bits: 110
  • Default mask: /24
  • Hosts/network: 254

params.label: Class C 192.168.1.0/24 — leading bits 110, first octet 192–223

hack.memory: Class C = Compact / Consumer. 192–223, /24, leading bits 110. 254 hosts — perfect for a house, a coffee shop, a small office.
hack.practice: Is 192.168.50.1 Class C? Yes. Is 224.0.0.5 Class C? No — it's Class D multicast. Is 223.255.255.255 Class C? Yes (top of range).
hack.meta: Your home router uses Class C private (192.168.x.x). The /24 default is burned in by daily use.

1.6.b.info:
Class D covers first octets 224–239, identified by the leading binary pattern 1110 (1110xxxx). Class D is reserved for multicast traffic — one-to-many delivery — and cannot be assigned to a host as a source IP.

Multicast groups: 224.0.0.5 (OSPF all routers), 224.0.0.6 (OSPF DR/BDR), 224.0.0.9 (RIPv2), 224.0.0.10 (EIGRP), 224.0.0.18 (HSRP v2), 224.0.0.102 (HSRP v1). The block 224.0.0.0/24 is 'link-local multicast' (never routed).

Multicast is used by routing protocols and streaming applications so that a single packet can reach many receivers without flooding the whole network. Subnet masks do not apply to Class D because there is no host portion — each Class D address is a group identifier.

  • Range: 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255
  • Leading bits: 1110
  • No default subnet mask — group IDs
  • Never assignable to a host NIC as source

hack.memory: Class D = Delivery to many (multicast). 224–239. OSPF hellos, EIGRP hellos, streaming all live here. No host can own one — they're group chat room IDs.
hack.practice: Memorize 3 multicast addresses: OSPF = 224.0.0.5/6, EIGRP = 224.0.0.10, HSRP v2 = 224.0.0.102.
hack.meta: Exam favorite: 'which address is a multicast?' — any 224.x–239.x.

1.6.b.info:
Class E covers first octets 240–255, leading binary pattern 1111 (1111xxxx). Class E is reserved for experimental / future use and has never been publicly allocated. Most OS stacks will refuse to let you assign a Class E address; some modern proposals (RFC drafts) suggest reclaiming it as extra IPv4 space, but no standard has ever done so.

Range: 240.0.0.0 – 255.255.255.255. The final address 255.255.255.255 is the limited broadcast address (never routed off-link), carved out separately.

For the exam: know the range, know it's experimental, know you won't see Class E addresses in any real network. It mostly shows up as a distractor.

  • Range: 240.0.0.0 – 255.255.255.255
  • Leading bits: 1111
  • Never publicly assigned
  • 255.255.255.255 = limited broadcast (carved out)

hack.memory: Class E = Experimental / Excluded. 240–255. Never on a real network. If you see a 241.x.x.x on the exam, it's a trick.
hack.practice: Distinguish: 239.255.255.255 = Class D (last multicast). 240.0.0.0 = Class E start. 255.255.255.255 = limited broadcast (special case).
hack.meta: Pure recognition question on exam. Know 240–255 = Class E.

1.6.b.info:
The first-octet ranges seem to march 1, 2, 3... all the way up to 126 for Class A — but then there's a gap at 127. The 127.0.0.0/8 block is not Class A despite the first octet falling in that range. It is reserved for loopback — traffic to any 127.x.x.x address never leaves the local device.

This is why every Class A chart reads 1–126, not 1–127. The 127 gap is pure Cisco exam bait — a question like 'Is 127.0.0.1 a Class A address?' wants you to say No, it's loopback.

The entire /8 (127.0.0.0 – 127.255.255.255) is loopback, not just 127.0.0.1. So 127.5.99.22 is also loopback and never generates network traffic.

  • 127.0.0.0/8 reserved for loopback (RFC 5735)
  • Not classified as Class A even though 127 is in the A range
  • Entire /8 block, not only 127.0.0.1

hack.memory: Class A stops at 126. 127 is a loopback gap. Remember: 'A ends at 126, 127 is a mirror.' The exam loves this trap.
hack.practice: When you recite class ranges, say 'Class A: 1–126 (127 is loopback), B: 128–191, C: 192–223.' Always include the parenthetical.
hack.meta: Guaranteed distractor on the exam.

1.6.b.info:
CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) was introduced by RFC 1518 and RFC 1519 in 1993 to replace the rigid classful allocation system. Classful had a fatal flaw: if a company needed 400 host addresses, a Class C (254 hosts) wasn't enough, so they were forced to take a Class B (65,534 hosts) — wasting ~65,000 addresses.

CIDR lets any mask length be used with any network. The /n prefix notation (e.g., 203.0.113.0/23) specifies exactly how many network bits, regardless of the first-octet class. Today, the Internet runs entirely on CIDR — BGP advertises CIDR prefixes, not class defaults.

CIDR also enables supernetting (combining multiple classful networks into one larger summary, e.g., two adjacent /24s summarized as one /23) and VLSM (variable-length subnet masks within an organization).

  • CIDR introduced: 1993
  • Replaces the classful A/B/C default masks
  • Enables VLSM and supernetting
  • Foundation of modern Internet routing (BGP)

hack.memory: CIDR = Classless. Break the class rules, pick any mask. Introduced 1993 to stop the massive address waste of classful.
hack.practice: Summarize 192.168.0.0/24 + 192.168.1.0/24 into one CIDR prefix: 192.168.0.0/23. That's CIDR supernetting in action.
hack.meta: The exam still tests classful concepts, but the real world is 100% CIDR.

1.6.c.info:
A subnet mask is a 32-bit value shaped like an IPv4 address but with a strict rule: it must have contiguous 1-bits on the left followed by contiguous 0-bits on the right. The 1-bits mark the network portion of the address; the 0-bits mark the host portion.

A valid mask never has a 0 followed by a 1. 255.255.255.0 (11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000) is valid. 255.0.255.0 (11111111.00000000.11111111.00000000) is invalid because there's a 0 before the second group of 1s.

This rule is why only 9 octet values can ever appear in a subnet mask: 0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255. Each represents a running sum of leading 1-bits. Memorizing these 9 values lets you recognize valid masks at a glance.

  • Must be contiguous 1s then contiguous 0s — no alternation
  • 9 valid octet values — memorize them
  • The mask AND the IP = network address

params.label: Valid mask format: 24 ones, then 8 zeros

hack.memory: Subnet mask = unbroken wall of 1s, then unbroken floor of 0s. No gaps, no islands. If you see a 0 before a 1 anywhere, the mask is fake.
hack.meta: Classic distractor question: 'pick the valid subnet mask.' Use the 9-value trick.

1.6.c.info:
CIDR slash notation expresses a subnet mask by the count of 1-bits. /24 means 24 leading 1s = 255.255.255.0. /16 = 16 ones = 255.255.0.0. /30 = 30 ones = 255.255.255.252. The valid range is /0 (default route, all bits host) through /32 (host route, all bits network).

CIDR notation is compact and universal — routers, route advertisements (BGP, OSPF, EIGRP), ACLs, and documentation all use /n. Every Cisco IOS command that accepts a mask in dotted-decimal form will also display CIDR equivalents.

On the exam, fluent conversion between CIDR and dotted-decimal is mandatory. A /26 must instantly be 255.255.255.192 and vice versa. This is the #1 skill drill for subnetting speed.

  • Counts leading 1-bits in the mask
  • /0 through /32 valid
  • /31 = RFC 3021 P2P, /32 = host route
  • Must convert both directions instantly

hack.memory: CIDR /n = count the 1-bits. /24 = 24 ones. /30 = 30 ones. Just counting.
hack.meta: Must be reflex-fast for exam speed.

1.6.c.info:
/24 = 255.255.255.0. Binary: 24 ones followed by 8 zeros. Block size 256 (full last octet). This is the Class C default mask and the most common subnet size in the world — virtually every home LAN, lab, and documentation example uses /24.

Each /24 subnet contains 256 total addresses, 254 usable hosts (minus network and broadcast). The network address is the .0; the broadcast is the .255; usable hosts run from .1 to .254.

Because the mask falls cleanly on an octet boundary, /24 math is trivial: the third octet identifies the network, the fourth octet identifies the host. No mental arithmetic required.

  • Binary: 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000
  • Block size: 256
  • Usable hosts: 254
  • Use cases: home LAN, VLAN, standard department

hack.memory: /24 = your home network. 256 addresses, 254 hosts. 192.168.1.0/24, 10.0.0.0/24 — ubiquitous.
hack.practice: List the network, first host, last host, broadcast for 10.1.1.0/24: 10.1.1.0 / 10.1.1.1 / 10.1.1.254 / 10.1.1.255.
hack.meta: Baseline for all subnetting. If /24 isn't automatic, nothing else will be.

1.6.c.info:
/25 = 255.255.255.128. Binary last octet: 10000000. Block size: 256 − 128 = 128. Splits a /24 into two equal /25 subnets.

Each /25 has 128 total addresses, 126 usable hosts. For a /24 parent like 192.168.1.0/24, the two /25 children are:

  • 192.168.1.0/25 — hosts .1–.126, broadcast .127
  • 192.168.1.128/25 — hosts .129–.254, broadcast .255

Common use: cut a /24 in half when you have two departments of ~100 hosts each.

hack.memory: /25 = half of a /24. 128 addresses, 126 hosts. Two /25s in every /24.
hack.practice: Find broadcast of 10.10.10.128/25: next block − 1 = 10.10.10.255. Hosts .129–.254.
hack.meta: The first step into real subnetting. /25 doubles as 'halve a /24.'

1.6.c.info:
/26 = 255.255.255.192. Binary last octet: 11000000. Block size: 256 − 192 = 64. Splits a /24 into four /26 subnets.

Each /26 has 64 total addresses, 62 usable hosts. Subnets inside a /24 parent (192.168.1.0/24):

  • 192.168.1.0/26 — hosts .1–.62, broadcast .63
  • 192.168.1.64/26 — hosts .65–.126, broadcast .127
  • 192.168.1.128/26 — hosts .129–.190, broadcast .191
  • 192.168.1.192/26 — hosts .193–.254, broadcast .255

Use when you need small LANs of ~50 hosts each (small office departments).

hack.memory: /26 = quarters of a /24. Block 64. 62 hosts. Four /26s per /24.
hack.practice: Which /26 is 192.168.1.200 in? 200 falls in the 192 block, so 192.168.1.192/26.
hack.meta: /26 is the most-tested non-default mask on the CCNA.

1.6.c.info:
/27 = 255.255.255.224. Binary last octet: 11100000. Block size: 256 − 224 = 32. Splits a /24 into eight /27 subnets.

Each /27 has 32 total addresses, 30 usable hosts. Subnet starts: 0, 32, 64, 96, 128, 160, 192, 224.

Use when departments are around 25–30 hosts (common for smaller VLANs).

hack.memory: /27 = block 32, 30 hosts. Eight subnets per /24. Count by 32s: 0, 32, 64, 96, 128, 160, 192, 224.
hack.practice: 10.0.0.100/27: block 32, 100 in 96 block. Network 10.0.0.96, broadcast 10.0.0.127, hosts .97–.126.
hack.meta: Very common exam mask. Memorize block 32 starts.

1.6.c.info:
/28 = 255.255.255.240. Binary last octet: 11110000. Block size: 256 − 240 = 16. Splits a /24 into sixteen /28 subnets.

Each /28 has 16 total addresses, 14 usable hosts. Subnet starts: 0, 16, 32, 48, 64, 80, 96, 112, 128, 144, 160, 176, 192, 208, 224, 240.

Use for small lab segments, guest Wi-Fi, management VLANs (usually <14 devices).

hack.memory: /28 = block 16, 14 hosts. Sixteen subnets per /24. Hex digit =4 bits → /28 is a natural 'hex boundary' in the mask.
hack.practice: 192.168.1.50/28: 50 is in the 48 block. Network 192.168.1.48, broadcast 192.168.1.63, hosts .49–.62.
hack.meta: Useful mental anchor: /28 lines up with hex nibbles.

1.6.c.info:
/29 = 255.255.255.248. Binary last octet: 11111000. Block size: 256 − 248 = 8. Splits a /24 into thirty-two /29 subnets.

Each /29 has 8 total addresses, 6 usable hosts. Use for very small segments — a small rack with 6 servers, a voice VLAN for a small office, or a group of infrastructure devices.

Subnet starts: 0, 8, 16, 24, 32... incrementing by 8.

hack.memory: /29 = block 8, 6 hosts. 'Six pack' subnet. Great for a tiny server group.
hack.practice: 10.1.1.17/29: 17 in the 16 block. Network 10.1.1.16, broadcast 10.1.1.23, hosts .17–.22.
hack.meta: Minimum useful host subnet for small LANs before /30 P2P territory.

1.6.c.info:
/30 = 255.255.255.252. Binary last octet: 11111100. Block size: 256 − 252 = 4. Each /30 has 4 total addresses, 2 usable hosts.

This is the standard mask for point-to-point router links: two routers on opposite ends of a serial, Ethernet, or fiber link need exactly 2 IPs — one each — plus the network and broadcast. A /30 fits perfectly.

Subnet starts: 0, 4, 8, 12, 16... by 4s. Example: 10.0.0.0/30 → hosts .1 and .2, broadcast .3.

hack.memory: /30 = router-to-router link. Block 4, 2 hosts. One for each router end. Everybody uses this for WAN circuits and OSPF adjacencies.
hack.practice: Configure interface s0/0/0 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252 on R1 and 10.0.0.2 /30 on R2. Ping across.
hack.meta: Exam guaranteed: 'which mask fits 2 hosts exactly?' = /30.

1.6.c.info:
/31 = 255.255.255.254. Binary last octet: 11111110. Only 2 total addresses, both usable (RFC 3021 exception). Normally you lose 2 for network and broadcast, but /31 on a point-to-point link allows both to be used as host addresses.

Why it works: P2P links don't need broadcast (there's only one other device to talk to), so the /31 standard redefines both addresses as usable. Saves 2 IPs per P2P link compared to /30 — at scale (thousands of links), this matters.

Not supported on multi-access media (Ethernet LAN with many hosts). Only use on true P2P serial, P2P Ethernet, or tunnel interfaces. Some old IOS versions refuse /31; modern Cisco fully supports it.

hack.memory: /31 = skinny P2P. Only 2 addresses, both usable. ISPs love it for saving IPs at scale.
hack.practice: Configure /31 on a serial link between two routers and verify OSPF still forms an adjacency.
hack.meta: Exam knows RFC 3021. 'Which mask conserves addresses on P2P links?' = /31.

1.6.c.info:
/32 = 255.255.255.255. Binary: all 32 bits are network. Only 1 address, 1 host — the address itself. Used for host routes and loopback interfaces on routers.

When you create a Cisco loopback (interface loopback 0), assigning a /32 is idiomatic: it consumes zero extra address space and gives the router a stable, always-up ID used for OSPF router ID, BGP peering, and management.

In the routing table, every IOS router installs a local (L) route for its own interface IPs as /32 entries — that's why you see L 192.168.1.1/32 is directly connected alongside the connected C 192.168.1.0/24.

hack.memory: /32 = a single pinpoint. Host route. One address. Loopback interfaces and specific-host entries use it.
hack.practice: interface loopback 0 / ip address 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.255. Verify with show ip interface brief.
hack.meta: Loopbacks and local routes = /32 territory. Know it.

1.6.d.info:
The block size method is the fastest manual technique for subnetting without binary conversions. Block size = 256 − (mask value in the interesting octet). The interesting octet is the one where the mask transitions from 1s to 0s.

For /26 (255.255.255.192): interesting octet is the 4th, mask value 192, block size = 256 − 192 = 64. Subnets start at multiples of the block size: 0, 64, 128, 192. That's four /26 subnets in one /24.

Memorize the mask-to-block-size chart for the last octet: 128→128, 192→64, 224→32, 240→16, 248→8, 252→4, 254→2, 255→1. The same numbers apply if the interesting octet is the third or second — the math doesn't change, only which octet you're operating on.

hack.memory: 256 minus the mask value = block size. That's the entire shortcut. Subnets start at multiples of the block.
hack.practice: Drill: /27 → block 32. /28 → block 16. /29 → block 8. /30 → block 4. Repeat until automatic.
hack.meta: This one trick solves 80% of subnetting questions on the exam.

1.6.d.info:
The interesting octet is the octet in the subnet mask where the boundary between network bits and host bits falls — the only octet that is neither 255 (fully network) nor 0 (fully host).

For /20 = 255.255.240.0, the interesting octet is the third octet (value 240). For /26 = 255.255.255.192, it's the fourth octet (192). For /8, /16, /24 — there is no interesting octet because the boundary falls cleanly on an octet edge (trivial math).

All subnetting arithmetic happens in the interesting octet only. Leave the other octets alone. That's why block size shortcuts work so fast.

hack.memory: Interesting = the octet that's neither 255 nor 0. Do all math there, ignore the rest.
hack.practice: Identify the interesting octet for /12 (2nd), /22 (3rd), /27 (4th), /9 (2nd), /25 (4th).
hack.meta: Isolating the interesting octet cuts subnetting to one variable.

1.6.d.info:
To find the network address of any IP+mask pair: round the IP's interesting octet down to the nearest multiple of the block size. Leave all other octets unchanged. Zero out any octets to the right of the interesting octet.

Example: 172.16.68.25/22. Mask = 255.255.252.0, interesting octet = third (value 252), block size = 256 − 252 = 4. Multiples of 4: 0, 4, 8... 64, 68, 72. The third octet 68 is already a multiple of 4, so network = 172.16.68.0/22. If it had been 70: round down to 68.

This is identical to the bitwise AND operation but faster for humans. Always double-check that octets to the right of the interesting octet are zero in the network address.

hack.memory: Round DOWN the interesting octet to a block-size multiple. Zero everything to the right. Done.
hack.practice: 10.50.200.15/20: block 16 in octet 2. Octet 2 value=50, nearest multiple of 16 ≤ 50 is 48. Network = 10.48.0.0/20.
hack.meta: The fundamental subnetting skill. Drill until it's reflex.

1.6.d.info:
Broadcast = (next network address) − 1. Or equivalently: take the network address and set every host bit to 1.

Example: network = 192.168.1.64/26, block size = 64, next network = 192.168.1.128, so broadcast = 192.168.1.127.

For masks where the interesting octet is not the last, the broadcast affects multiple octets. 172.16.68.0/22: next network = 172.16.72.0, so broadcast = 172.16.71.255. The host bits span octets 3 and 4, so both are filled with 1s.

hack.memory: Broadcast = next subnet minus 1. Or: flip all host bits to 1.
hack.practice: Find broadcast of 10.1.1.32/27: block 32, next subnet = 10.1.1.64, broadcast = 10.1.1.63.
hack.meta: Half of every subnet question asks for the broadcast.

1.6.d.info:
First usable host = network address + 1. The network address itself is reserved; the very next address is the first assignable host.

Example: network 192.168.1.64/26 → first usable = 192.168.1.65. Network 10.20.30.0/24 → first usable = 10.20.30.1 (this is why default gateways are so often .1).

Except for /31 (2 usable, both endpoints) and /32 (1 address, itself), every mask skips the network address.

hack.memory: First host = network + 1. Simple. The default gateway usually lives here.
hack.practice: Network 172.16.0.128/25 → first host 172.16.0.129. Network 10.0.0.192/26 → first host 10.0.0.193.
hack.meta: Often the default gateway, so this number appears in every lab config.

1.6.d.info:
Last usable host = broadcast address − 1. The broadcast is reserved; the address just below it is the last assignable host.

Example: broadcast 192.168.1.127 → last usable = 192.168.1.126. Broadcast 10.20.30.255 → last usable = 10.20.30.254.

Knowing first + last usable instantly tells you the full usable range in the subnet — essential for DHCP pool planning and ACL configuration.

hack.memory: Last host = broadcast − 1. One step below the shout-to-everyone address.
hack.practice: Subnet 10.1.1.96/27: broadcast .127, last host 10.1.1.126. Range .97–.126.
hack.meta: The DHCP pool's upper bound. Routinely appears in simulation labs.

1.6.e.info:
Number of subnets = 2^b where b is the number of bits borrowed from the host portion beyond the classful default mask. Each additional borrowed bit doubles the number of subnets.

Example: Class C default is /24. Extend to /26 (borrow 2 bits): 2^2 = 4 subnets. Extend to /27 (borrow 3 bits): 2^3 = 8 subnets. Extend to /28 (borrow 4 bits): 2^4 = 16 subnets. The pattern doubles each step.

For Class A (default /8) subnetted to /16: borrow 8 bits → 2^8 = 256 subnets. For Class B (/16) to /24: 8 bits → 256 subnets. The formula is class-agnostic — what matters is bits borrowed, not the starting class.

hack.memory: Subnets = 2 to the borrowed. Each borrowed bit doubles the count.
hack.practice: From /16 to /20: 4 borrowed bits → 2^4 = 16 subnets. From /24 to /30: 6 borrowed → 64 subnets.
hack.meta: First half of every 'how many subnets / how many hosts' exam question.

1.6.e.info:
Usable hosts per subnet = 2^h − 2, where h is the number of host bits remaining after the mask. The −2 accounts for the network address (all host bits 0) and the broadcast address (all host bits 1), neither assignable.

Example: /26 mask has 6 host bits → 2^6 − 2 = 64 − 2 = 62 usable hosts. /28 has 4 host bits → 2^4 − 2 = 14 hosts. /30 has 2 host bits → 2^2 − 2 = 2 hosts (P2P).

Exception: /31 uses RFC 3021 rules and has 2 usable hosts (no −2). /32 has 1 (host route). Every other mask applies the −2 penalty.

hack.memory: Hosts = 2 to the leftover host bits, minus 2. Minus 2 for network + broadcast.
hack.meta: This column is the most repeated fact on CCNA subnetting questions.

1.6.e.info:
Borrowed bits are bits moved from the host portion to the network portion to create more subnets. The number of bits borrowed = (new mask length) − (default classful mask length).

Example: starting from Class C default /24, choosing /27 means borrowing 3 bits (27 − 24). Each borrowed bit doubles the subnet count and halves the hosts.

This concept predates CIDR (it comes from classful-era thinking), but it still powers the subnets formula: 2^borrowed = number of subnets. On the exam, if a question frames itself 'from a Class C, how many subnets with a /28 mask?' — you know immediately 4 bits borrowed = 16 subnets.

params.label: Class C /27 — 3 bits borrowed from host portion

hack.memory: Borrowed = new mask − default class mask. Those borrowed bits feed the 2^b subnet formula.
hack.practice: /17 from Class A default /8: 9 bits borrowed → 2^9 = 512 subnets. /20 from Class B /16: 4 borrowed → 16 subnets.
hack.meta: Wendell Odom OCG uses 'borrowed bits' language in every subnetting chapter.

1.6.e.info:
The Class C host-count column is the single most-tested subnet fact on the CCNA. Memorize it cold:

  • /25 → 126 hosts (2^7 − 2)
  • /26 → 62 hosts (2^6 − 2)
  • /27 → 30 hosts (2^5 − 2)
  • /28 → 14 hosts (2^4 − 2)
  • /29 → 6 hosts (2^3 − 2)
  • /30 → 2 hosts (2^2 − 2)

Every number is roughly half the previous minus a bit. Used to answer 'given a required host count, what's the smallest mask?' questions — the most common VLSM prompt on the exam.

hack.memory: 126, 62, 30, 14, 6, 2 — tattoo this on your brain. Each is ~half the previous minus 2.
hack.practice: Timed drill: say the mask, partner says the host count. /28? 14. /26? 62. Go!
hack.meta: You will see this exact table on 3–5 exam questions, guaranteed.

1.6.e.info:
Given a host requirement, you must find the smallest power of 2 ≥ (hosts + 2). The +2 accounts for the network and broadcast addresses you'll lose. Then pick the mask that leaves exactly that many host bits.

Example: 'I need 50 usable hosts per subnet.' Try /26: 2^6 − 2 = 62 (fits!). /27: 2^5 − 2 = 30 (too few). Answer = /26.

'I need 100 usable hosts.' /25: 2^7 − 2 = 126 (fits). /26: 62 (too few). Answer = /25.

Never round down — if the requirement is 62 hosts, /26 gives exactly 62; but 63 hosts needs /25. Common trap: students forget the −2 penalty and pick one mask too small.

states: Pick mask leaving n host bits

hack.memory: Never round DOWN hosts. Always pick the next mask up. Remember the −2 (network + broadcast).
hack.meta: Reverse-lookup questions are the exam's favorite VLSM trap.

1.6.f.info:
VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Mask) means using different mask lengths within the same parent network so each subnet is right-sized to its actual host requirement. Without VLSM, you'd have to pick one mask and apply it everywhere, wasting addresses on small segments and running out on large ones.

Example: a single /24 organization needs one 100-host LAN + one 50-host LAN + four 2-host P2P links. With VLSM: use a /25 for 100, /26 for 50, and four /30s for the P2Ps. Without VLSM (say a fixed /25 everywhere): only 2 subnets — can't fit all the different sizes.

VLSM requires a classless routing protocol that carries the mask in its updates: OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2, BGP. Classful protocols (RIPv1, IGRP) can't carry the mask, so all subnets had to share one fixed mask — a huge limitation that CIDR and VLSM fixed in the 1990s.

hack.memory: VLSM = custom-fit subnets. Big subnet for a big LAN, small subnet for a P2P link. No one-size-fits-all waste.
hack.practice: From 10.10.10.0/24, carve a /25 (for 100 hosts), /26 (for 50), /27 (for 30), and the rest as /30 P2P links. Document each network/broadcast.
hack.meta: VLSM simulation questions are common. Practice the allocation process until it's mechanical.

1.6.f.info:
The golden rule of VLSM allocation: always carve out the largest subnet first, then the next largest, and so on down to the smallest. This prevents fragmentation — small subnets don't land inside would-be large subnet boundaries.

Why: if you allocate a /30 first from a /24 parent, then try to find a /25 later, the /30 you already placed might be inside the /25's range, forcing you to skip that block. Allocating big first means the remaining space is always contiguous and predictable.

Step-by-step: (1) list all required subnets with host counts, (2) sort descending by size, (3) compute the mask for each, (4) allocate in order starting at the base network address, (5) each new subnet begins right after the previous subnet's broadcast address.

states: Next subnet starts after previous broadcast

hack.memory: Biggest first. Like packing a suitcase — shoes first, socks last.
hack.practice: Given 5 subnet requirements (100, 50, 25, 10, 2 hosts), sort descending before allocating any. Never skip the sort.
hack.meta: Exam simulations grade this: wrong order = wrong network addresses = fail.

1.6.f.info:
When allocating VLSM subnets, two rules protect the integrity of the plan:

  • No overlaps — two subnets cannot share any addresses. Overlaps cause routing loops, ARP confusion, and silent traffic misdelivery.
  • Each new subnet starts at the address immediately after the previous subnet's broadcast. This creates a dense, contiguous allocation with no wasted address space.

Example: /25 at 192.168.1.0 (broadcast .127). Next subnet (say /26) starts at 192.168.1.128 (broadcast .191). Next (/27) starts at 192.168.1.192 (broadcast .223). No gaps, no overlaps.

Gaps are fine as reserves (leave room for future growth) but overlaps are always wrong. If you ever write two subnets whose address ranges intersect, the plan is broken.

hack.memory: End of one = start of next. Broadcast of previous + 1 = network of next. No doubling up.
hack.practice: Plan a VLSM on paper. Every address gets one (and only one) label. If two labels overlap, redo it.
hack.meta: Overlap detection is often an exam scenario: 'which subnet is misconfigured?' — find the one that overlaps.

1.6.f.info:
VLSM is only possible when the routing protocol in use carries the subnet mask in each routing update. Protocols that do this are called classless: OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2, IS-IS, BGP.

Without the mask in the update, the receiver has to assume the classful default (a /8, /16, or /24 based on the first octet), which breaks VLSM entirely. A receiver seeing 172.16.32.0 with no mask would assume /16 — missing any /20 or /24 subnets within.

All modern routing protocols are classless. Classful protocols (RIPv1, IGRP) are obsolete and not used in production. The CCNA covers OSPF, EIGRP, and static routing — all classless — so VLSM just works.

hack.memory: Classless = carries the mask = VLSM works. Classful = assumes default = VLSM fails.
hack.practice: Configure OSPF between two routers with VLSM subnets: /25, /26, /30. All appear correctly in show ip route with their actual masks.
hack.meta: Exam asks 'which protocols support VLSM?' — OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2. Not RIPv1.

1.6.f.info:
RIPv1 (RFC 1058, 1988) and IGRP (Cisco proprietary, 1980s) are classful routing protocols — they do not include the subnet mask in routing updates. Both assume the classful default mask for any network they advertise.

Consequence: neither supports VLSM, neither supports discontiguous subnets, neither is used in modern networks. RIPv1 has been replaced by RIPv2 (RFC 2453, 1998), which is classless. IGRP was replaced by EIGRP in the 1990s.

For the exam: know that these are classful, know they fail on VLSM, and know their classless successors. You won't be asked to configure them.

hack.memory: RIPv1 and IGRP = dinosaurs. Classful, no VLSM. Their classless replacements (RIPv2, EIGRP) fixed the gap.
hack.practice: Remember: RIPv1 ≠ RIPv2. Version 2 is classless. Version 1 is not.
hack.meta: Trivia-level. The exam asks once, the real world never does.

1.6.f.info:
A /30 subnet (255.255.255.252) gives exactly 2 usable host addresses — perfect for a point-to-point link between two router interfaces. One IP for each end, plus the always-reserved network and broadcast.

Point-to-point links are everywhere in enterprise and ISP networks: serial WAN circuits, router-to-router Ethernet, OSPF/EIGRP adjacencies, MPLS PE-CE connections. Using a /30 (rather than a /24 or /29) conserves precious address space at scale — an ISP with 10,000 P2P links saves 250,000+ addresses by using /30 instead of /29.

Modern alternative: /31 (RFC 3021) gives 2 usable addresses out of 2 total — zero waste. But /30 remains the common, conservative default because it works on every platform and OS without issues.

hack.memory: /30 = 2 usable = perfect for a router-to-router link. Block size 4, 2 hosts, you'll see this everywhere.
hack.practice: Design a topology with 5 routers in a line, each link a /30. That's 4 /30 subnets. Draw it out with network/host IPs.
hack.meta: Exam guaranteed: 'which mask for 2 hosts on a P2P?' = /30 (or /31).

1.6.g.info:
Inside an 8-bit octet, each position has a fixed value: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. These are powers of 2 from 2^7 down to 2^0. They always sum to 255.

Reading an octet in binary: the bit at each position contributes its value (if 1) or nothing (if 0). This is the foundation of all binary-to-decimal conversion.

Write these 8 numbers across the top of your whiteboard the moment you sit down for the exam. They are your cheat sheet — every subnetting question reduces to these 8 numbers.

hack.memory: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Halve each time. Sum = 255. Write it down FIRST thing on the exam.
hack.practice: Say the sequence out loud 3 times every day: '128-64-32-16-8-4-2-1.' Make it muscle memory.
hack.meta: Foundational. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 12 drills this specifically.

1.6.g.info:
To convert an 8-bit binary number to decimal: add the positional values of every 1-bit.

Example: 11001010 → 128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 202.

Example: 11111111 → 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 255.

Example: 10000000 → just 128.

This is the single most-drilled skill for CCNA subnetting. Faster you are, more time you have for other exam questions.

hack.memory: Sum the 1-bits using 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Ignore the 0-bits. Done.
hack.practice: Convert 10 random 8-bit binaries per day. Target under 10 seconds each.
hack.meta: This speed separates passers from non-passers.

1.6.g.info:
To convert a decimal number (0–255) to 8-bit binary: subtract the largest fitting positional value, mark a 1 at that position, and repeat.

Example: 202 → fits 128 (202 − 128 = 74, mark bit 7 = 1), fits 64 (74 − 64 = 10, bit 6 = 1), doesn't fit 32 (bit 5 = 0), doesn't fit 16 (bit 4 = 0), fits 8 (10 − 8 = 2, bit 3 = 1), doesn't fit 4 (bit 2 = 0), fits 2 (2 − 2 = 0, bit 1 = 1), doesn't fit 1 (bit 0 = 0) → 11001010.

Always write all 8 bits including leading zeros. In networking, the octet's bit positions are fixed — a leading zero can't be dropped.

hack.memory: Subtract biggest fits, mark 1s. Always write 8 bits (leading zeros included).
hack.practice: Convert: 193 = 11000001. 224 = 11100000. 77 = 01001101. Verify each by adding back.
hack.meta: Pair this with binary→decimal drills for symmetry.

1.6.g.info:
Only 9 numbers can ever appear in a valid subnet mask octet: 0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255. Each adds one more leading 1-bit:

  • 00000000 = 0
  • 10000000 = 128
  • 11000000 = 192
  • 11100000 = 224
  • 11110000 = 240
  • 11111000 = 248
  • 11111100 = 252
  • 11111110 = 254
  • 11111111 = 255

If an exam asks 'which is a valid mask?' and the answer choices include 255.255.255.131 — instantly know it's invalid (131 isn't in the list).

hack.memory: 0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255. That's it. Any other value in a mask octet = fake mask.
hack.practice: Pick 10 valid masks and 10 invalid, shuffle, and identify each. Should take 5 seconds per item.
hack.meta: Fast mask validation is a 1-second exam skill.

1.6.g.info:
Every hexadecimal digit represents exactly 4 bits (a nibble). Hex uses 16 symbols (0–9 and A–F) to cover the 16 possible 4-bit patterns. This is why hex is the preferred shorthand for MAC addresses (48 bits = 12 hex digits) and IPv6 (128 bits = 32 hex digits).

Conversion table:

  • 0 = 0000, 1 = 0001, 2 = 0010, 3 = 0011
  • 4 = 0100, 5 = 0101, 6 = 0110, 7 = 0111
  • 8 = 1000, 9 = 1001, A = 1010, B = 1011
  • C = 1100, D = 1101, E = 1110, F = 1111

Two hex digits = 1 octet. So CA = 1100 1010 = 202 decimal. MAC address 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E is 48 bits = 12 hex digits = 6 octets.

hack.memory: 1 hex digit = 4 bits (a nibble). 2 hex digits = 1 byte. 8 hex digits = IPv4 address. 32 hex digits = IPv6 address.
hack.practice: Convert AF to binary: 1010 1111 = 175. Convert 11110000 to hex: F0 = 240.
hack.meta: Needed for IPv6 and for reading show commands.

1.6.g.info:
The six hex letters extend decimal's 0–9: A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15. These map directly to 4-bit binary values 1010 through 1111.

Mnemonic: 'After 9, the Alphabet' — A is 10, B is 11, all the way to F = 15. Because 15 = 1111 (four 1-bits), F is the 'maxed-out nibble.'

Used constantly in MAC addresses (aaaa.bbbb.cccc), IPv6 (fe80::1), and OSPF/EIGRP debug output.

hack.memory: A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15. After 9, count by letters.
hack.practice: What's 0xCA in decimal? C=12, A=10 → 12×16 + 10 = 202.
hack.meta: Pair with IPv6 work — you'll use this daily.

1.6.g.info:
Two-digit hex to decimal: (left digit × 16) + (right digit). Each hex digit position is a power of 16 (just like each decimal digit is a power of 10).

Example: 0xCA → (C × 16) + A = (12 × 16) + 10 = 192 + 10 = 202.

Example: 0xFF → (F × 16) + F = (15 × 16) + 15 = 240 + 15 = 255. Which makes sense: 11111111 is both 0xFF and 255.

For 4-digit hex (16-bit IPv6 hextet): treat each digit as a power of 16, so 2001 = (2×4096) + (0×256) + (0×16) + 1 = 8193.

hack.memory: Left digit × 16, plus right digit. 0xCA = 12×16 + 10 = 202.
hack.practice: Convert 0x80, 0xC0, 0xE0, 0xF0 to decimal: 128, 192, 224, 240. (These are your subnet-mask values in hex.)
hack.meta: Useful when reading debug output and IPv6 addresses.

1.6.h.info:
show ip interface brief (often shortened to sh ip int br) produces a compact table listing every interface on the device with its IP address, administrative state, and line/protocol state. It is the first command you run whenever something isn't working.

Column meanings:

  • Interface — the physical or virtual interface name (Gi0/0, Vlan1, Lo0)
  • IP-Address — the configured IPv4 address, or 'unassigned'
  • OK? — YES/NO, indicates IP address validity
  • Method — manual, DHCP, TFTP, unset
  • Status — Layer 1 state: up, down, administratively down
  • Protocol — Layer 2 state: up or down

The two state columns map to OSI: Status = L1 (is the cable/link present?), Protocol = L2 (has the data link layer come up?). Healthy interfaces show up / up. Admin down / down means someone typed shutdown. Up / down means the cable is present but L2 negotiation failed (encapsulation mismatch, wrong clock rate, etc.).

hack.memory: sh ip int br = the attendance sheet. Status = L1 (cable). Protocol = L2 (link). Both up = healthy.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, create 3 interfaces, shut one, break one with wrong clock rate, leave one healthy. Run sh ip int br and identify each state.
hack.meta: First command on every exam simulation. Fluent reading is mandatory.

1.6.h.info:
show ip route displays the full IPv4 routing table — every network the router knows how to reach. For each entry you see:

  • Route source code (C, L, S, O, D, R, etc.) — how the route was learned
  • Destination network / mask
  • Administrative distance / metric in brackets, like [110/20]
  • Next-hop IP or exit interface
  • Age — when the route was last updated

Example line: O 10.0.2.0/24 [110/20] via 10.0.0.2, 00:15:22, Serial0/0 — learned via OSPF, AD 110, metric 20, next hop 10.0.0.2, 15 minutes old, reachable through Serial0/0.

Always scan the table top-to-bottom to understand the router's worldview. Missing routes are the most common cause of 'why can't I ping?' issues.

hack.memory: show ip route = GPS map. Every destination the router can reach. Missing destination = no route = packet dropped.
hack.practice: Configure static routes on a 3-router topology, then read the output to confirm every destination network has an entry.
hack.meta: Simulations often ask 'why can R1 not reach 10.0.2.0?' — answer is always 'no route.'

1.6.h.info:
C marks a directly connected route — a network reachable through an interface on this router. The router adds a C route automatically whenever an interface has an IP address and comes up/up. You cannot manually create connected routes; they appear and disappear with interface state.

Connected routes have an administrative distance (AD) of 0, the lowest possible. Nothing beats a C route — the router trusts its own interfaces absolutely.

If you configure ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0 on Gi0/0 and no shutdown, you'll see:
C 192.168.1.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0

hack.memory: C = Connected. Auto-added, AD 0. Most trusted route on the router. Disappears the moment the interface goes down.
hack.practice: Bring an interface up, see the C route. Shut it down, watch the C route disappear. Re-enable, watch it return.
hack.meta: Baseline route type. Every routing table has C routes.

1.6.h.info:
L marks a local route — a /32 host route for the router's own interface IP address. IOS 15+ installs both a C route (for the network) and an L route (for the specific IP) on every connected interface.

Example: with ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0 on Gi0/0, the table shows:
C 192.168.1.0/24 is directly connected, Gi0/0
L 192.168.1.1/32 is directly connected, Gi0/0

Purpose: the L route lets the router answer ICMP, ARP, and routing-protocol traffic destined for its own IP efficiently — the forwarding engine sees an exact /32 match rather than parsing the /24.

hack.memory: L = Local = router's own IP as /32. Comes paired with every C route in IOS 15+.
hack.practice: Configure an interface, run show ip route, confirm both the C and the L entries.
hack.meta: New since IOS 15 (~2010). Don't be surprised when you see /32 next to every interface.

1.6.h.info:
S marks a static route — a route manually configured by an administrator via the ip route command. Static routes have an administrative distance of 1 (second only to connected).

Example configuration: ip route 10.0.2.0 255.255.255.0 10.0.0.2. The router installs:
S 10.0.2.0/24 [1/0] via 10.0.0.2

Static routes are simple, predictable, and use no CPU/bandwidth (no protocol chatter). They are perfect for stub networks, default routes, and small topologies. Downside: no automatic failover — if the next hop goes down, the route stays in the table but traffic is black-holed unless you add a backup.

hack.memory: S = Static = admin-configured. AD 1. Simple, trustworthy, no auto-failover.
hack.practice: Configure ip route 10.0.2.0 255.255.255.0 10.0.0.2 and verify with show ip route.
hack.meta: Static routes appear in nearly every CCNA lab.

1.6.h.info:
These three letters mark dynamically learned routes:

  • O = OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) — AD 110. Industry-standard link-state protocol.
  • D = EIGRP (Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol). 'D' because EIGRP's algorithm is DUAL (Diffusing Update Algorithm). AD 90 (internal).
  • R = RIP (Routing Information Protocol) — AD 120. Simple distance-vector, largely obsolete in production.

Why different letters? 'E' was already claimed by EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol, pre-BGP era), so EIGRP got 'D' for its algorithm. These codes appear in show ip route output for every dynamically learned route.

The CCNA exam focuses on OSPF and a brief awareness of EIGRP and RIP. Expect to identify each by its code letter and AD.

hack.memory: O = OSPF, D = EIGRP (Dual algorithm), R = RIP. AD: O=110, D=90, R=120.
hack.practice: Configure OSPF between 2 routers and look for 'O' in show ip route. Note the AD 110 in brackets.
hack.meta: Dynamic route identification is always on the exam.

1.6.h.info:
S* marks a candidate default route — a static route to 0.0.0.0/0 (the default / 'match anything' destination). The asterisk means this route is the current 'gateway of last resort' for unmatched traffic.

Configuration: ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1. The router installs:
S* 0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 203.0.113.1

When a packet's destination doesn't match any specific route, the router uses the S* default. This is how every home/SOHO network reaches the Internet — one default route toward the ISP.

You can also have dynamically learned defaults (from DHCP, OSPF default-information, RIP) — they'd appear with their own letters (O*, R*, etc.). Only one default can be active at a time per VRF.

nodes: Forward to gateway of last resort

hack.memory: S* = static default = gateway of last resort. 0.0.0.0/0 means 'everything not otherwise listed.'
hack.practice: Configure ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 10.0.0.1 and watch S* appear in the routing table.
hack.meta: Every Internet-connected router has one. Exam favorite.

1.6.h.info:
ping sends ICMP Echo Request packets to a destination and waits for ICMP Echo Reply responses. It tests Layer 3 reachability end-to-end.

Cisco IOS output symbols:

  • ! — reply received (success)
  • . — timeout (no reply)
  • U — destination unreachable (ICMP error received)
  • ? — unknown packet
  • M — fragmentation needed but DF bit set

A typical successful output: !!!!! (5 replies, 100% success rate). A typical failure: ..... (all timeouts). Mixed !!.!! indicates intermittent loss.

Use ping at every step of the troubleshooting process: ping the default gateway first, then a remote subnet, then external services. If any step fails, you know where the problem starts.

hack.memory: Ping = knock on the door. ! = answered. . = silence. U = 'you can't reach here' note.
hack.practice: From R1, ping R3's loopback. If it fails, ping R2 first. Work hop by hop.
hack.meta: #1 troubleshooting tool. Every simulation uses it.

1.6.h.info:
traceroute (Cisco, Linux, macOS) and tracert (Windows) show every router hop along the path from source to destination, with round-trip times for each. It's invaluable for finding exactly where packets drop or where latency spikes.

How it works: traceroute sends probes with incrementing TTL values starting at 1. Each router that decrements TTL to 0 sends back an ICMP Time Exceeded message, revealing its identity. When the destination finally replies with an ICMP Echo Reply (or Port Unreachable, on Windows UDP-based tracert), the trace ends.

Reading output: each line is one hop. Three RTT values per hop (from 3 probes). Asterisks (*) mean no response from that hop (often because ICMP is filtered). Use asymmetric-route thinking — a missing hop doesn't always mean a broken hop; it might just not reply.

hack.memory: Traceroute = flashlight through the tunnel. TTL trick reveals every router in sequence.
hack.practice: From a PC, tracert 8.8.8.8. Count hops. Note where latency jumps — that's usually the ISP handoff.
hack.meta: Exam asks 'which command shows the path?' — traceroute (Cisco/Unix) or tracert (Windows).

1.7.a.info:
The 10.0.0.0/8 block is the largest of the three RFC 1918 private ranges. It spans 10.0.0.0 through 10.255.255.255, providing 16,777,214 usable host addresses (2^24 − 2) in a single flat network or up to millions of subnets via VLSM.

Because the first octet (10) is fixed and the remaining 24 bits are available, it's the ultimate canvas for subnetting. A large enterprise can carve /16 blocks per site, /24 blocks per VLAN, and /30s per WAN link — all within 10.x.x.x without ever running out of address space.

Any address starting with 10 is always private. Internet backbone routers drop any packet with a 10.x.x.x source or destination. To reach the Internet, hosts with 10.x.x.x addresses must be translated via NAT or PAT.

  • First octet fixed = 10
  • 24 host bits = ~16.7M addresses
  • In binary, prefix is 00001010
  • Falls within Class A (1–126), but entirely reserved as private

hack.memory: 10 = TEN = The Enterprise Network. One /8 to rule them all. 16.7 million hosts.
hack.practice: Is 10.255.255.1 private? Yes. Is 11.0.0.1 private? No (Class A public). Is 10.0.0.0 usable host? No (network address).
hack.meta: Most common private range in production. Drill recognition until instant.

1.7.a.info:
The 10.0.0.0/8 block is the dominant internal range for large enterprises, data centers, cloud providers, and SDN fabrics. Its size (16.7M addresses) means a global company can assign every office, rack, VLAN, and VM without address conflicts.

Typical design patterns:

  • Per-site /16: 10.1.0.0/16 = Site 1, 10.2.0.0/16 = Site 2. 256 sites per /8.
  • Per-VLAN /24: 10.1.10.0/24 = Site 1 VLAN 10, 10.1.20.0/24 = Site 1 VLAN 20.
  • Per-P2P link /30: 10.99.99.0/30 = first backbone link.

Cloud providers embrace 10/8 heavily. AWS VPC default is 10.0.0.0/16. Azure VNet default uses 10.x.x.x. CoreWeave's internal and tenant networks are built on 10/8. Kubernetes pod networks default to 10.244.0.0/16 (Flannel) or 10.42.0.0/16 (k3s).

hack.memory: Enterprise = 10/8. AWS, Azure, CoreWeave, Kubernetes — they all camp out here.
hack.practice: Design a 3-site enterprise using 10/8: pick /16s for each site, /24s for VLANs, /30s for links.
hack.meta: Real-world context that helps the concept stick.

1.7.a.info:
RFC 1918 addresses (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) are explicitly non-routable on the public Internet. Backbone routers (Tier 1 ISPs) are configured with ACLs or prefix lists that drop any packet with an RFC 1918 source or destination IP.

Consequence: a host with a private IP cannot communicate with a public Internet host unless the private IP is translated to a public IP at the network boundary. This translation is done by NAT (Network Address Translation), almost always in its PAT variant (many-to-one), typically on the edge router or firewall.

Why drop these at the edge? Because millions of separate organizations all use the same private ranges. If ISPs routed them, a packet destined for '10.1.1.1' could arrive at any of millions of devices — chaos. Dropping them forces organizations to translate at the boundary, guaranteeing uniqueness on the wire.

hack.memory: Private IP can't survive on the public Internet. NAT is the bouncer swapping IDs at the door.
hack.practice: Check your home router: inside LAN is 192.168.x.x, outside WAN is a public IP. NAT does the swap.
hack.meta: 'Why can't private IPs reach the Internet without NAT?' — common exam concept.

1.7.b.info:
The middle RFC 1918 range is 172.16.0.0/12, running from 172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255. That's 1,048,574 usable host addresses (2^20 − 2) — about 1 million.

The trap: the range is not 172.0.0.0–172.255.255.255. It's just the 16 consecutive /16 blocks from 172.16 through 172.31. Anything outside that second-octet window (172.15 or 172.32) is public.

The /12 mask means the first 12 bits are fixed. Second octet in binary: 0001xxxx — the 4 free bits yield 0000 (16) through 1111 (31). That's where the 16–31 boundary comes from.

  • Range: 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255
  • /12 = 12 fixed bits
  • ~1,048,574 hosts
  • 16 /16 networks inside it

hack.memory: 172.16 through 172.31. Sweet 16, not over 31. The /12 is the tricky private range.
hack.meta: Most-missed private range on CCNA. Drill the boundary.

1.7.b.info:
The /12 prefix means the first 12 bits of the address are fixed. For 172.16.0.0: in binary 10101100.00010000. The first 8 bits are the first octet (172), then the first 4 bits of the second octet (0001) are also fixed at 0001 — that's what locks the second octet between 16 and 31.

Second octet has 4 free bits (the right 4 of the 8): 0000 (value 16), 0001 (17), 0010 (18)... up to 1111 (31). That's 16 possible values — the 16 allowed /16 networks inside 172.16.0.0/12.

If the 5th bit of the second octet flips to 1, the address becomes 10101100.0010xxxx = 172.32 or higher — outside the /12, and thus public.

params.label: 172.31.255.255 — last address in the /12 (next bit would exit)

hack.memory: /12 = 12 fixed bits = 4 free bits in octet 2 = values 16–31. Binary proof: 0001xxxx.
hack.practice: Write the binary for 172.16, 172.20, 172.31, 172.32. Confirm the /12 boundary breaks at 172.32.
hack.meta: Binary proof helps rock-solid memorization.

1.7.b.info:
172.15.x.x is PUBLIC. It falls below the 172.16 start of the private /12 range. This is a classic exam trap because many students assume 'anything starting with 172 is private' — false.

Range audit: 172.15.255.255 is the last public address before the private block begins. 172.16.0.0 is the first private address.

Always check the full second octet against the 16–31 window before declaring a 172.x.x.x address private.

hack.memory: 172.15 = PUBLIC (too early). 172.16 = start of private.
hack.practice: Is 172.15.5.1 private? NO. Is 172.16.0.1 private? YES. Drill 10 edge cases daily.
hack.meta: Trap question: 'which of these is NOT private?' — 172.15.x.x is the distractor.

1.7.b.info:
172.32.x.x is PUBLIC. It's one /16 past the end of the private /12 range. Students who remember 'starts at 172.16' but forget 'ends at 172.31' often pick 172.32.x.x as private — wrong.

Range audit: 172.31.255.255 is the last private address. 172.32.0.0 is the first public address after.

Both ends of the 172.16/12 range are favorite exam targets. Always double-check the second octet is between 16 and 31 inclusive.

hack.memory: 172.32 = PUBLIC (too late). 172.31 = end of private.
hack.practice: Is 172.32.5.1 private? NO. Is 172.31.255.254 private? YES.
hack.meta: Pair with the 172.15 edge case — drill both ends of the /12.

1.7.b.info:
Docker's default bridge network is 172.17.0.0/16 — a /16 block that falls inside the 172.16/12 private space. Every container on the default bridge gets a 172.17.x.x IP by default.

If you install Docker on your workstation and run docker network inspect bridge, you'll see this exact subnet. This occasionally conflicts with corporate VPNs that also use 172.16–172.31 internally, requiring admins to reconfigure Docker's default pool.

Other Docker-created networks pick subsequent /16 blocks: 172.18.0.0/16, 172.19.0.0/16, etc. — always within the RFC 1918 /12.

hack.memory: Docker = 172.17/16. Useful context; not heavily exam-tested.
hack.practice: On any machine with Docker: docker network inspect bridge and read the Subnet field.
hack.meta: Real-world flavor. Not required, but memorable.

1.7.c.info:
The Class C private range is 192.168.0.0/16, covering 192.168.0.0 through 192.168.255.255. That's 65,534 usable hosts (2^16 − 2) — identical in size to a classful Class B default.

Although written as one /16, in practice it's subnetted into 256 /24 blocks (192.168.0.0/24 through 192.168.255.0/24), each with 254 hosts. Consumer routers almost always pick one /24 (192.168.1.0/24 or 192.168.0.0/24) as the LAN default.

The entire 192.168.x.x space is private. 192.168.0.1 through 192.168.255.254 are all assignable hosts (within their /24 sub-blocks).

hack.memory: 192.168 = home router. 65K addresses, usually used as 256 /24 subnets. Familiar from every Wi-Fi setup.
hack.practice: Is 192.168.0.0 private? Yes. Is 192.168.255.255 private? Yes (broadcast of 192.168.255.0/24). Is 192.167.1.1 private? No.
hack.meta: Easiest private range to memorize — you use it daily.

1.7.c.info:
Almost every consumer router — Linksys, Netgear, TP-Link, D-Link, Asus, Eero — ships with one of these LAN defaults: 192.168.0.0/24 or 192.168.1.0/24, gateway at .1.

When you connect to home Wi-Fi and run ipconfig (Windows) or ifconfig/ip addr (Linux/macOS), you'll typically see your machine with an address like 192.168.1.42, subnet 255.255.255.0, gateway 192.168.1.1.

This uniformity is why the range feels so familiar. It also means attackers assume 192.168.1.1 is the default gateway for initial probing — part of why many security-conscious users change the default LAN subnet.

  • Linksys / Cisco consumer: 192.168.1.0/24
  • Netgear: 192.168.1.0/24
  • D-Link: 192.168.0.0/24
  • TP-Link: 192.168.0.0/24

hack.memory: 192.168.1.1 = home router login IP. Burned into every home network on the planet.
hack.practice: Check your own laptop right now. What's your IP and default gateway? Odds are 192.168.x.x / .1.
hack.meta: Familiar range = easy exam question.

1.7.c.info:
The private boundary is exact at the second octet. Only 192.168.x.x is private. 192.169.x.x is PUBLIC, even though 192 is the Class C start.

Common exam trap: you know 192.168 = private, and assume nearby prefixes (192.167, 192.169, 192.170) are also private. They're not. Only the exact 192.168 second-octet value is reserved.

Similarly: 192.167.255.255 = public. 192.168.0.0 = private. 192.168.255.255 = private. 192.169.0.0 = public. Tight as a ruler.

hack.memory: Only 192.168 is private. 192.167 = public, 192.169 = public. Exact match required.
hack.practice: Cover the full octet and check: 192.167.1.1 (public), 192.168.1.1 (private), 192.169.1.1 (public).
hack.meta: Frequent distractor on CCNA. Tight boundary awareness wins the point.

1.7.d.info:
RFC 1918 (published February 1996, titled 'Address Allocation for Private Internets') formally defined the three private IPv4 address ranges and the rules for their use. It is one of the most-cited RFCs in networking.

The three ranges:

  • 10.0.0.0/8 — 16,777,214 hosts (Class A range)
  • 172.16.0.0/12 — 1,048,574 hosts (Class B range)
  • 192.168.0.0/16 — 65,534 hosts (Class C range)

Rule: these addresses are not routable on the public Internet. Backbone ISPs drop packets with RFC 1918 source or destination. To reach the Internet, private addresses must be translated via NAT or PAT at the network boundary.

The exam expects you to recognize all three ranges instantly and know they're all defined in RFC 1918 (not to confuse with RFC 3927 for APIPA or RFC 3021 for /31 P2P).

hack.memory: RFC 1918 = 10, 172.16, 192.168. Three private ranges. One RFC. Core trivia.
hack.practice: Recite all three ranges with their CIDR prefixes in one breath. Drill until it's instant.
hack.meta: Direct 'name the RFC' question is common.

1.7.d.info:
NAT (Network Address Translation) rewrites IP addresses (and sometimes ports) as packets cross a network boundary. Its primary purpose is to let devices with private (RFC 1918) addresses communicate with the public Internet using a smaller pool of public addresses.

Three NAT flavors on CCNA:

  • Static NAT — 1:1 fixed mapping. One private IP always ↔ one public IP.
  • Dynamic NAT — 1:1 from a pool. First-come basis. Exhausts when pool fills.
  • PAT / NAT Overload — many-to-one using source port to differentiate. What home routers do.

Interfaces must be marked: ip nat inside on the LAN side, ip nat outside on the WAN side. Without both markings, NAT never triggers. Classic troubleshooting: 'NAT isn't working' → check for missing interface markings.

nodes: ip nat inside → NAT Router ← ip nat outside

hack.memory: NAT = ID swap at the border. LAN side = ip nat inside. WAN side = ip nat outside. Forget either → nothing happens.
hack.practice: Configure PAT on a router and watch show ip nat translations fill up as PCs browse.
hack.meta: Configuration and verification of NAT is a 1.7 exam pillar.

1.7.d.info:
Static NAT creates a permanent one-to-one mapping between a private (inside local) address and a public (inside global) address. Every packet from the private host goes out with the same public IP; every packet to the public IP comes back to the same private host.

Used when an internal server must be reachable from the Internet at a known, stable address — web servers, mail servers, VPN concentrators, game servers.

Command:

ip nat inside source static 10.1.1.5 203.0.113.5
interface g0/0
 ip nat inside
interface g0/1
 ip nat outside

Now 10.1.1.5 and 203.0.113.5 are permanently linked. Anyone on the Internet hitting 203.0.113.5 lands on 10.1.1.5. 10.1.1.5's outbound packets always appear to come from 203.0.113.5.

hack.memory: Static = 1:1 forever. Bolted together. Used for servers needing stable public IPs.
hack.practice: Configure static NAT for a web server. Test by browsing from an outside device to the public IP — you should reach the inside server.
hack.meta: Exam will ask you to configure and recognize static NAT lines.

1.7.d.info:
Dynamic NAT assigns public IPs from a pool on a first-come-first-served basis. Each internal host gets a 1:1 mapping temporarily; when the host stops transmitting, the mapping times out and the public IP returns to the pool.

Problem: if all pool addresses are in use, new hosts can't communicate until someone's mapping ages out. Modern networks use PAT (many-to-one) instead of pure dynamic NAT for this reason.

Commands:

access-list 1 permit 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255
ip nat pool PUBPOOL 203.0.113.10 203.0.113.20 netmask 255.255.255.0
ip nat inside source list 1 pool PUBPOOL

Here, internal hosts matching ACL 1 get translated to a random IP from the pool 203.0.113.10–20.

hack.memory: Dynamic = 1:1 rental from a pool. Runs out if everyone talks at once. Rare in real networks; common on exam.
hack.practice: Configure dynamic NAT with a pool of 3 addresses and 5 hosts trying to use it. Watch some get blocked.
hack.meta: Dynamic NAT appears more on exam than in the real world. Know the commands.

1.7.d.info:
PAT (Port Address Translation), also called NAT Overload, maps many private addresses to a single public address by tracking unique source port numbers for each session. This is what your home router does and what most enterprise edge routers do for outbound Internet traffic.

Mechanism: when host 10.0.0.5 (source port 5000) sends a packet to 8.8.8.8:443, the router rewrites the source to 203.0.113.1:17234 (a unique public port) and remembers the mapping. The reply comes back to 203.0.113.1:17234, and the router translates it back to 10.0.0.5:5000. Multiple internal hosts share the single public IP, distinguished only by port number.

Command (most common form):

access-list 1 permit 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255
ip nat inside source list 1 interface GigabitEthernet0/1 overload
interface g0/0
 ip nat inside
interface g0/1
 ip nat outside

The key word is overload. Without it, you'd have dynamic NAT (1:1 from pool).

hack.memory: PAT = many hosts, one IP, unique ports. Keyword: overload. This is home router magic.
hack.practice: Configure PAT on a Packet Tracer router. Run 3 PCs browsing simultaneously. Check show ip nat translations — all mapped to the one outside IP with different ports.
hack.meta: Most commonly implemented NAT type. Guaranteed exam question.

1.7.d.info:
Inside Local = the private IP of an internal host as seen from inside the network. This is the 'real' address configured on the host's NIC — usually a 10/172.16/192.168 RFC 1918 address.

Example: a web server inside the LAN has IP 10.1.1.10. That's its Inside Local address. From the server's perspective, every packet it sends or receives uses this address.

Mnemonic for all four NAT address terms: the first word (Inside / Outside) = which side of the NAT boundary the device physically lives on. The second word (Local / Global) = which address space the address belongs to (Local = private, Global = public).

hack.memory: Inside Local = REAL INSIDE IP. The private address configured on the host. 'Local' = private realm.
hack.practice: Draw a 4-quadrant diagram: (Inside, Local) (Inside, Global) (Outside, Local) (Outside, Global). Fill in with a concrete example.
hack.meta: The 4-quadrant diagram is the single best CCNA NAT study aid.

1.7.d.info:
Inside Global = the public IP that an internal host is translated to — the address that Internet hosts see when the inside host communicates outward.

Example: the inside host 10.1.1.10 (Inside Local) is translated by NAT to 203.0.113.5 (Inside Global). When that host browses Google, Google's server sees the source as 203.0.113.5, not 10.1.1.10.

Key: Inside Global is still describing an inside host — just as the outside world sees it. Same physical device, different IP label depending on perspective.

hack.memory: Inside Global = INSIDE HOST AS THE INTERNET SEES IT. Same host, public address label.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure PAT and run show ip nat translations. Identify Inside Local (private) and Inside Global (public) columns.
hack.meta: Exam tests this language precisely — memorize the four terms.

1.7.d.info:
Outside Local = the IP of an external host as it appears from inside the network. In most straightforward NAT scenarios, this equals the Outside Global (the real public IP of the external host) because there's no second NAT layer between them.

Outside Local differs from Outside Global only in advanced scenarios involving twice NAT or overlapping address spaces, where the internal network has to treat the outside host as if it had a different IP (for example, because two companies merged and both use the same private range).

For CCNA, you'll usually see Outside Local = Outside Global. Know the concept, know they can differ in corner cases, and move on.

hack.memory: Outside Local = OUTSIDE HOST AS LOCAL SEES IT. Usually identical to Outside Global. Corner case for twice NAT.
hack.practice: Focus on Inside Local/Global for the exam — Outside Local/Global mostly matter in twice-NAT edge cases.
hack.meta: Lower-weight term but still tested as a distractor.

1.7.d.info:
Outside Global = the actual public IP of an external host. This is the real address assigned to that remote server on the Internet — e.g., 8.8.8.8 for Google's DNS.

Unlike Outside Local (which is how the outside host's address appears from inside, possibly translated), Outside Global is the ground-truth public IP. In simple NAT, they're the same.

The four NAT terms together form a complete picture of any NAT transaction: Inside Local (real private), Inside Global (translated public), Outside Local (remote as seen locally), Outside Global (remote's actual public IP).

hack.memory: Outside Global = REMOTE HOST'S REAL PUBLIC IP. The true Internet identity of the far-end device.
hack.practice: Fill out all 4 terms for a transaction: 10.1.1.10 browsing to 142.250.80.46 (Google).
hack.meta: Complete the 4-term mental map for fluency.

1.7.d.info:
show ip nat translations displays the current NAT translation table — every active mapping the router is tracking. For each entry you see:

  • Pro — protocol (tcp, udp, icmp)
  • Inside global — translated public IP and port
  • Inside local — real private IP and port
  • Outside local — external IP as seen locally
  • Outside global — real external IP

Example: tcp 203.0.113.5:17234 10.1.1.10:5000 8.8.8.8:443 8.8.8.8:443 — a PAT entry for a browse session.

Related commands:

  • show ip nat statistics — pool usage, hit counts, inside/outside interface summary
  • clear ip nat translation * — flush all dynamic entries (forces re-translation)
  • debug ip nat — live translation events (use sparingly, high CPU)

hack.memory: show ip nat translations = the live NAT ledger. Every entry = one active mapping.
hack.practice: After configuring PAT, generate traffic from 2–3 inside hosts and run the show command. Identify each column.
hack.meta: Almost guaranteed exam simulation command.

1.7.e.info:
APIPA (Automatic Private IP Addressing) uses the range 169.254.0.0/16 (defined in RFC 3927 as the IPv4 link-local block). When a Windows or macOS host is configured for DHCP but receives no DHCP Offer, it self-assigns a random address from 169.254.1.0 – 169.254.254.255 (first and last /24 reserved).

The host performs ARP probes on the random address to detect conflicts before claiming it. Once assigned, it can communicate with other link-local hosts on the same physical segment — but cannot reach any other subnet or the Internet.

APIPA is strictly a fallback mechanism. Its purpose is to allow small ad-hoc networks (a couple of laptops plugged into a switch with no DHCP) to still communicate without manual configuration. In any production environment, seeing APIPA = DHCP failure.

  • Range: 169.254.0.0/16
  • Assignable: 169.254.1.0 – 169.254.254.255
  • Link-local only — never routed
  • RFC 3927 (IPv4 link-local)

hack.memory: 169.254 = APIPA = DHCP failed. The host couldn't reach a DHCP server, so it self-assigned.
hack.practice: Unplug your ethernet cable from a DHCP-dependent network. Watch the NIC drop to 169.254.x.x after a minute.
hack.meta: Instantly recognizable exam clue. 169.254 = DHCP broken.

1.7.e.info:
