If you see a 169.254.x.x address on a client, the message is unambiguous: the host tried DHCP and got no response.

Possible root causes:

  • DHCP server is down or out of addresses
  • DHCP relay (ip helper-address) missing or misconfigured on the router between client and server
  • Physical disconnection (cable unplugged, port shut down, switch port blocked by STP)
  • VLAN mismatch (client in wrong VLAN, DHCP traffic not reaching server)
  • DHCP snooping dropping offers

First step: check physical layer (link light). Second step: check DHCP server logs. Third step: check ip helper-address on the client's default gateway if the DHCP server is on a different subnet.

states: PC boots and sends DHCP Discover

hack.memory: 169.254 on a host = DHCP BROKEN. First check cable, then DHCP server, then relay.
hack.practice: Configure a switch port in VLAN 99 where no DHCP server reaches. Plug a PC in and watch it drop to APIPA.
hack.meta: #1 most-asked APIPA exam question: 'what does 169.254 mean?' Answer: DHCP failure.

1.7.e.info:
APIPA addresses (169.254.0.0/16) are link-local only. Routers will not forward them — the packets never leave the physical segment. A device with an APIPA address cannot reach another VLAN, another building, the default gateway's other interfaces, or the Internet.

APIPA is designed for minimal ad-hoc connectivity — two laptops plugged into the same unmanaged switch can communicate if both have APIPA addresses in the same /16. That's basically it.

Similar to IPv6 link-local (fe80::/10), but IPv6 link-local is mandatory and used for routing protocols; IPv4 APIPA is a fallback after DHCP failure.

left.label: Can reach
items: Other APIPA hosts on same segment

hack.memory: APIPA = one-segment-only island. Routers refuse to forward 169.254.x.x packets.
hack.practice: From an APIPA host, try to ping an Internet address. Result: always fails (and always will).
hack.meta: Exam: 'Can a 169.254.x.x host reach google.com?' — No.

1.7.e.info:
APIPA is a Microsoft Windows feature (since Win98) that was adopted in RFC 3927 and later implemented in macOS and Linux (via avahi or similar). On client OSes, it activates automatically when a DHCP lease fails.

Cisco routers and switches do not use APIPA. If a Cisco interface has no IP configured, it simply has no IP — it does not self-assign 169.254.x.x. This is a common exam distractor: 'will a Cisco router with no DHCP lease use APIPA?' — No.

Linux is selective — most distros default to not using APIPA unless avahi-autoipd or similar is installed and enabled.

hack.memory: APIPA = client OS fallback. Windows/macOS yes, Cisco IOS no.
hack.practice: On a Cisco router, remove the IP from an interface. It becomes 'unassigned' — never 169.254.
hack.meta: Exam trap: Cisco devices don't use APIPA.

1.7.e.info:
When a host shows 169.254.x.x, follow a structured troubleshoot:

  1. Physical: is the cable plugged in? Link light on? Switch port not shut?
  2. Switch port: is the port in the correct VLAN? Is it err-disabled? (Check show interfaces status.)
  3. DHCP server: is it running? Out of leases? DHCP server logs show any Discover arriving?
  4. DHCP relay: if server is on a different subnet, is ip helper-address configured on the client's gateway?
  5. Retry: Windows ipconfig /release then ipconfig /renew. Linux sudo dhclient -r then sudo dhclient.

Most often the answer is #1 or #4 — physical layer or missing ip helper-address.

hack.memory: APIPA troubleshoot order: cable → port → server → relay → renew.
hack.practice: Break each step in Packet Tracer: unplug cable, misconfig VLAN, remove ip helper-address, and watch APIPA appear each time.
hack.meta: DHCP troubleshoot is a classic simulation task.

1.7.f.info:
The entire 127.0.0.0/8 block (127.0.0.0 through 127.255.255.255) is reserved for loopback. Any packet with a destination in this range never leaves the originating device; the TCP/IP stack loops it back up to the application layer on the same host.

That's over 16 million addresses reserved for a function that, in practice, uses only one address (127.0.0.1). The entire /8 is kept reserved for historical reasons and to simplify the stack's handling of loopback traffic.

Notably: 127.0.0.0/8 falls inside the Class A first-octet range (1–127), but it is not considered a Class A network — it's a separate loopback reservation.

  • Range: 127.0.0.0 – 127.255.255.255
  • Full /8 reserved; only 127.0.0.1 commonly used
  • Never generates wire traffic
  • Carved out from Class A space

hack.memory: 127/8 = loopback island. All 16M addresses reserved. Only 127.0.0.1 gets real work. No wire traffic ever.
hack.practice: Ping 127.5.5.5 on your laptop — it works! The whole /8 is loopback, not just .0.0.1.
hack.meta: Exam trick: 'is 127.100.1.1 loopback?' Yes.

1.7.f.info:
127.0.0.1 — nicknamed localhost — is the most-used loopback address. Pinging it (ping 127.0.0.1) tests the local TCP/IP stack: if the ping succeeds, the NIC driver, IP layer, ICMP, and protocol stack are all working on the local machine. If it fails, the local stack is broken — hardware, NIC driver, or OS issue, not network.

In application development, 127.0.0.1 is used constantly: web servers, databases, and services bind to 127.0.0.1 so they're only reachable from the local machine (not the network). For example, http://localhost:3000 resolves to 127.0.0.1:3000.

In /etc/hosts (Linux/macOS) or C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts (Windows), 127.0.0.1 localhost is the first entry. That's why 'localhost' always resolves to 127.0.0.1.

hack.memory: 127.0.0.1 = localhost = self. Pinging it tests your own TCP/IP stack. Dev work lives here.
hack.practice: Ping 127.0.0.1. Curl http://localhost:3000 against any local dev server. Both use 127.0.0.1.
hack.meta: Every developer uses this daily.

1.7.f.info:
A common exam trap: students remember 127.0.0.1 as loopback but forget the rest of the /8. In reality, every address from 127.0.0.0 through 127.255.255.255 is loopback.

Exam-style trick: 'Which of the following is a loopback address? A) 127.0.0.1 B) 127.5.5.5 C) 127.100.100.100 D) All of the above.' Correct answer: D.

Rarely, 127.0.0.2+ is used by local application configs to distinguish different services, though this is uncommon. The whole range is reserved and never routed.

hack.memory: 127.ANYTHING = loopback. Not just .0.0.1. The whole /8.
hack.practice: Ping 127.99.88.77 on your machine. It works — loopback handles the whole range.
hack.meta: Classic exam distractor: 'which of these is loopback?' — all 127.x options are.

1.7.f.info:
Do not confuse the loopback address (127.0.0.1, a specific IP) with a loopback interface on a Cisco router (a virtual interface you create with interface loopback 0).

A Cisco loopback interface is a virtual L3 interface that is always up/up. You assign it a real routable IP (not 127.0.0.1 — usually a /32 from a reserved block like 1.1.1.1/32 for R1, 2.2.2.2/32 for R2, etc.). It's used for:

  • OSPF / BGP router ID — a stable identifier that doesn't depend on a physical interface going down
  • Management endpoint — SSH/Telnet target that's always reachable via any path
  • BGP peering — with update-source loopback 0, peering survives link failures

Example:

interface loopback 0
 ip address 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.255
 no shutdown

That's a routable /32 (not 127.x.x.x), used as a stable router identity.

hack.memory: Loopback ADDRESS = 127.x.x.x (host self-test). Loopback INTERFACE = virtual Cisco interface with any routable IP.
hack.practice: Configure interface loopback 0 / ip address 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.255 on a router. show ip interface brief shows it as up/up with 1.1.1.1 — nothing to do with 127.x.x.x.
hack.meta: Exam distinguishes these two loopback concepts precisely.

1.7.f.info:
A classic bottom-up troubleshooting sequence using ping isolates exactly where connectivity breaks:

  1. ping 127.0.0.1 — tests the local TCP/IP stack. If fail, the OS or NIC driver is broken.
  2. ping (own IP) — tests the local NIC. If fail, the NIC is misconfigured or the cable is bad.
  3. ping (default gateway) — tests Layer 2 reachability to the gateway. If fail, a local LAN/VLAN problem.
  4. ping (remote host on another subnet) — tests full Layer 3 routing path.
  5. ping 8.8.8.8 — tests Internet reachability / NAT.
  6. ping google.com — tests DNS resolution on top of everything else.

The first step that fails tells you the layer with the problem. Stop there and investigate.

hack.memory: Ping ladder: loopback → self → gateway → remote → Internet → DNS. First failure = problem layer.
hack.practice: Run the full sequence from your laptop now. If anything fails unexpectedly, that's a real diagnostic you just learned.
hack.meta: Foundational troubleshooting methodology. Every support engineer uses this.
